section.title: Sing to the Lord a New Song
section.sectionRationale: An enthronement hymn whose lines were sung by David when the ark entered Jerusalem, this psalm summons all the earth, not Israel alone, to sing a new song to the reigning LORD and to await His coming to judge the world in righteousness, a hope fulfilled in Christ's global reign and return.
section.thesis: The LORD who made the heavens, unlike the worthless idols of the nations, deserves a new song from all the earth, and every family of peoples is summoned to worship Him because He is coming to judge the world in righteousness and truth.
section.frameNote: Three movements: a threefold call to sing a new song and declare God's glory among the nations, verses 1-6; a summons for the families of the peoples to ascribe Him glory and worship in holiness, verses 7-9; and creation's joy at the LORD's coming reign of righteous judgment, verses 10-13.

units.label: Sing to the Lord, all the earth
units.note: a call to declare His glory among the nations

verses.text: Sing to the LORD a {{chadash|new}} song; sing to the LORD, all the earth.
Sing to the LORD, bless His name; proclaim His salvation day after day.
Declare His {{kavod|glory}} among the nations, His wonders among all peoples.
For great is the LORD, and greatly to be praised; He is to be feared above all gods.
For all the gods of the nations are idols, but it is the LORD who made the heavens.
Splendor and majesty are before Him; strength and beauty fill His sanctuary.

units.label: Ascribe to the Lord glory and strength
units.note: a summons to the families of the peoples

verses.text: Ascribe to the LORD, O families of the nations, ascribe to the LORD glory and strength.
Ascribe to the LORD the glory due His name; bring an offering and enter His courts.
Worship the LORD in the splendor of His holiness; tremble before Him, all the earth.

units.label: He is coming to judge the earth
units.note: creation's joy at the LORD's righteous reign

verses.text: Declare among the nations: "The LORD {{malak|reigns}}!" The world is firmly established; it cannot be moved; He will judge the peoples with equity.
Let the heavens be glad and the earth rejoice; let the sea resound, and all that fills it.
Let the fields exult, and all that is in them. Then all the trees of the forest will sing for joy
before the LORD, for He is coming, He is coming to judge the earth. He will judge the world in righteousness and the peoples in His faithfulness.

chadash.gloss: New, fresh. A new song is called for whenever God acts in fresh salvation; Revelation 5:9 pictures the redeemed singing just such a new song to the Lamb who was slain.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weight, or honor. God's kavod, His weighty splendor, is to be declared among every nation, not confined to Israel; the New Testament shows this glory now radiating from Christ, John 1:14.

malak.gloss: To reign, be king. The declaration 'the LORD reigns' anchors this whole cluster of psalms; Revelation 11:15 answers it directly, 'the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ.'

basic.context: This psalm, whose language David used when the ark was brought to Jerusalem, calls not just Israel but all the earth and every family of nations to sing a new song to the LORD. Unlike the powerless idols of the nations, He alone made the heavens, and He is coming to judge the world with perfect righteousness.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm's global scope, 'all the earth,' 'the nations,' 'all peoples,' is not merely poetic exaggeration but a genuine missionary summons rooted in the promise to Abraham that all families of the earth would be blessed, fulfilled at last in the gospel going to every nation.
basic.originalLanguages: The call is for a chadash, new, song, sung whenever God acts in fresh salvation. His kavod, glory, is to be declared among the nations, not hoarded by Israel alone. And the announcement that the LORD malak, reigns, anchors the whole psalm's confidence in His coming righteous judgment.
basic.typology: The 'new song' anticipates the new song of the redeemed before the Lamb's throne, Revelation 5:9; the summons for 'all peoples' to worship anticipates the ingathering of every nation into Christ's kingdom; and the LORD's coming to judge the earth anticipates Christ's return as Judge.
basic.christ: Romans 15:9-12 gathers texts like this psalm to show that Christ came so that the Gentiles might glorify God for His mercy; the declaration 'the LORD reigns' finds its fulfillment when 'the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ,' Revelation 11:15, and His coming to judge the world anticipates Christ's return as the righteous Judge, Acts 17:31.
basic.god: God is Creator of the heavens, unlike the powerless idols of the nations, worthy of glory and strength from every family of peoples, and the coming Judge whose righteous rule will finally set creation itself singing in joy rather than groaning under the curse.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 15:9-12 quotes psalms like this one to show Christ came to bring the Gentiles to glorify God. Revelation 5:9 pictures the redeemed from every nation singing a new song to the Lamb. Revelation 11:15 answers this psalm's cry that 'the LORD reigns.' Acts 17:31 shows God has fixed a day to judge the world through the Man He has appointed.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's summons for all the earth and every family of nations to sing a new song to the reigning LORD runs forward to the day when people from every tribe and tongue gather around the Lamb's throne, and to Christ's certain return to judge the world in perfect righteousness.

context.literary: Part of the collection of enthronement psalms in Book IV, this psalm's opening lines closely parallel the song David appointed for the ark's arrival in Jerusalem, 1 Chronicles 16:23-33, structured as a triple call to sing, a rejection of idols, a summons to the nations, and a closing vision of creation's joy at God's coming judgment.
context.historical: Likely reused and treasured in the post-exilic community of Book IV, this psalm reminded scattered Israelites, without a king on David's throne, that the LORD Himself still reigns and that His worship was never meant to be confined to one people or one land.
context.canonical: A key text for biblical missiology, showing that God's global purpose for the nations did not begin in the New Testament but flows from Israel's own hymnbook, and a foundational enthronement psalm whose language of the LORD's reign and coming judgment recurs across Psalms 93-99.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To summon Israel and, through Israel, every nation on earth to sing fresh praise to the LORD as the only true God and coming Judge, in contrast to the worthless idols of the peoples.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific act of worship connected to Israel's history with the ark, yet it prescribes for every generation and every nation an abiding pattern: fresh, joyful song, rejection of idolatry, and confident hope in God's righteous judgment.

debates.question: Does verse 13, 'He is coming to judge the earth,' point to a past historical act, or forward to a final, still-future judgment?

views.label: The verse chiefly celebrates a past or repeated act of God's righteous rule over history
views.case: In its original setting, the psalm may reflect God's ongoing governance and vindication of His people within history, not only a single final event.

views.label: The verse points forward to God's final, universal judgment of all the earth
views.case: The scope, 'the world,' 'the peoples,' 'in righteousness,' exceeds any single historical event, and the New Testament treats this coming judgment as still future, fixed to a day, Acts 17:31.

debates.bearing: Both readings cohere: the God who has always ruled and vindicated His people in history is the same God who will bring final, universal judgment through Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The chadash, new, song called for in verse 1 is sung whenever God acts in fresh salvation. His kavod, glory, verse 3, is to be declared to every nation, not withheld from them. And the declaration that the LORD malak, reigns, verse 10, is the confident center around which the whole psalm turns.

typology.type: The new song sung to the LORD, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Revelation 5:9 pictures the redeemed of every tribe, tongue, people, and nation singing a new song before the Lamb's throne.

typology.type: The LORD's reign declared among the nations, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: Revelation 11:15 announces that the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of the Lord and of His Christ.

study.christ: Paul cites psalms of this kind in Romans 15:9-12 to show that Christ's mission always aimed at the nations glorifying God for His mercy. This psalm's central cry, 'the LORD reigns,' is answered decisively when heaven declares that 'the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ,' Revelation 11:15, and its promise that God is 'coming to judge the earth' anticipates Christ's appointed role as Judge of the living and the dead, Acts 10:42, 17:31.
study.god: God alone made the heavens, unlike the idols of the nations who are nothing, and He deserves glory and strength from every family of peoples on earth. He is also the coming Judge whose righteous rule will finally set the heavens, the sea, the fields, and the trees themselves singing for joy.

crossReferences.note: Records David's original song for the ark's arrival in Jerusalem, whose lines this psalm closely parallels.
Paul gathers Old Testament texts like this psalm to show Christ came so the Gentiles might glorify God for His mercy.
Pictures the redeemed from every nation singing a new song to the Lamb who was slain.
Declares the fulfillment of this psalm's cry, 'the LORD reigns,' in the kingdom of Christ.

study.oneStory: This psalm's summons for all the earth to sing a new song to the reigning LORD, and its hope that He is coming to judge the world in righteousness, is fulfilled in Christ, before whose throne the redeemed of every nation will one day sing, and who will return to judge the world exactly as this psalm promises.

sermon.title: Declare His Glory Among the Nations
sermon.why: Preached directly from Psalm 96 and tagged by Desiring God as Scripture Psalm 96, Piper traces the psalm's own call to declare God's glory among the nations and summon them to sing, showing how this global vision is fulfilled in the church's mission and in Christ's coming kingdom.

quiz.q: Who is called to sing a new song to the LORD in verse 1?

options.text: All the earth, not Israel alone.
options.note: The psalm's opening summons is explicitly universal, 'sing to the LORD, all the earth.'

options.text: Only the priests serving in the temple.
options.note: The call goes far beyond the priesthood to all the earth.

options.text: Only the king and his royal court.
options.note: The psalm addresses all the earth, not a royal court alone.

options.text: Only the descendants of Abraham.
options.note: Israel is included, but the psalm's scope explicitly reaches beyond Israel to all the earth and every nation.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe the gods of the nations in contrast to the LORD?

options.text: The gods of the nations are idols, but the LORD made the heavens.
options.note: Verse 5 draws a sharp contrast between powerless idols and the LORD who is Creator of the heavens.

options.text: The gods of the nations are lesser but still genuinely powerful rivals to the LORD.
options.note: The psalm calls them idols, worthless things, not genuine rival powers.

options.text: The gods of the nations share equally in creating the world with the LORD.
options.note: The psalm attributes creation of the heavens to the LORD alone.

options.text: The gods of the nations are simply different names for the same LORD.
options.note: The psalm treats them as idols, worthless, not as alternate names for the true God.

quiz.q: What are the families of the nations summoned to bring and do in verses 7-9?

options.text: Ascribe glory and strength to the LORD, bring an offering, and worship Him in the splendor of holiness.
options.note: These verses call the families of the nations to ascribe glory, bring an offering, enter His courts, and worship in holiness.

options.text: Bring their own idols to be blessed by the LORD.
options.note: The psalm calls the nations away from idols, not to bring idols for blessing.

options.text: Pay tribute exclusively to the king of Israel.
options.note: The summons is directed to the LORD Himself, not to Israel's human king.

options.text: Build a new temple for the LORD in their own lands.
options.note: The text calls for offerings and worship in His courts, not for building new temples elsewhere.

quiz.q: What declaration is to be made among the nations in verse 10?

options.text: "The LORD reigns!"
options.note: This central declaration anchors the psalm's confidence in God's stable, righteous rule over the whole world.

options.text: "The world will soon be destroyed."
options.note: The psalm declares the LORD's stable reign and coming righteous judgment, not the world's destruction.

options.text: "Israel alone will be spared judgment."
options.note: The declaration is about the LORD's universal reign, not about Israel's exemption from judgment.

options.text: "The nations must appoint their own judges."
options.note: The psalm assigns judgment to the LORD Himself, not to human judges appointed by the nations.

quiz.q: How does creation respond to the LORD's coming to judge the earth in verses 11-13?

options.text: The heavens, earth, sea, fields, and trees are pictured rejoicing and singing for joy.
options.note: The psalm personifies creation itself celebrating the LORD's coming righteous judgment.

options.text: Creation is pictured trembling in terror with no joy at all.
options.note: Trembling appears earlier at God's holiness, verse 9, but verses 11-13 emphasize gladness and joyful song.

options.text: Creation remains entirely silent and unmoved.
options.note: The text explicitly describes joyful, active response from creation, not silence.

options.text: Only the sea responds, while the land remains unaffected.
options.note: The heavens, earth, sea, fields, and trees are all named as responding, not the sea alone.

questions: The psalm calls for a 'new song,' fresh praise for fresh acts of God's salvation. What has God recently done in your life that deserves a genuinely new song of thanks, rather than routine praise?

This psalm insists that the idols of the nations are worthless while the LORD alone made the heavens. What modern 'idols,' wealth, status, control, quietly compete for the worship only God deserves in your life?
The families of the nations are summoned to worship 'in the splendor of His holiness.' What would it look like for your own worship to hold together both reverent awe and joyful gladness?
This psalm's global vision, 'declare His glory among the nations,' flows into the New Testament's call to make disciples of all nations. Where is God calling you to be part of that same declaration?

The psalm ends with creation itself rejoicing that the LORD is coming to judge the world in righteousness. How does hoping in Christ's certain, righteous return change how you respond to injustice you see today?

translationNotes.note: Much of this psalm's wording closely parallels the song David appointed to be sung when the ark of the covenant was brought into Jerusalem, recorded in 1 Chronicles 16:23-33.
The Hebrew word rendered 'idols' can carry the sense of 'worthless things' or 'nothings,' sharpening the contrast with the LORD who alone made the heavens.
The repetition 'He is coming, He is coming' in the Hebrew doubles for emphasis, underscoring the certainty of the LORD's coming judgment of the earth.

meta.notes: The local scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error in this sandbox, so the BSB text was obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/96.htm, cross-checked against the section map's 13-verse bound. Footnote markers were stripped and the em dash in verse 13 of the source was converted to a comma. Sermon link for John Piper's 'Declare His Glory Among the Nations' was verified this session via web_fetch of desiringgod.org, which tags the message's Scripture as Psalm 96.
